applicant_information.name: Laura M. Hernandez
applicant_information.school_dept: East Madison High School – Special Education Department

reason_for_request.coronavirus_pandemic_description_of_spec:
I am requesting assistance because the lingering financial impact of the coronavirus pandemic has continued to affect my household in multiple, overlapping ways, and we have reached a point where a relatively small, short-term, no‑interest loan would help us stabilize and avoid falling further behind.

During the height of the pandemic (2020–2022), my husband, Daniel, was laid off twice from his job in restaurant management as different restaurants closed or drastically reduced staff. Even after he found more stable employment in late 2022 in a warehouse position, his income never fully recovered to pre‑pandemic levels. We relied heavily on credit cards and payment plans during those years to cover basic living expenses (utilities, groceries, car repairs, and medical co‑pays), assuming things would normalize more quickly. Instead, we accumulated a balance that we are still paying off at high interest.

At the same time, we had significant unexpected costs related to schooling and childcare during remote and hybrid learning. Our two children, Mateo (age 9) and Sofia (age 6), were at home for long stretches while I was teaching online from our small apartment. We had to purchase an additional laptop, better internet equipment, and noise‑canceling headphones just so I could teach and they could attend their virtual classes. We also paid a neighbor to help supervise them during the hours when I was live on Zoom with my students. None of these expenses were fully reimbursed, and they contributed to the debt we are still carrying.

The pandemic also had a direct impact on our health and income. In early 2021, I contracted COVID‑19 and was out of work for several weeks. While I did use sick days, not all related medical costs were covered. I developed lingering respiratory issues that required multiple follow‑up appointments and inhalers, which came with co‑pays and prescription costs that we put on a credit card. My husband also had a mild case later that year and missed work, which, at the time, was unpaid for several of the days.

The most immediate issue now is that some of the deferred bills and pandemic‑era payment arrangements are coming due at the same time. For example, we entered into a deferred payment plan with our utility company in 2021 when Daniel was unemployed. That plan recently ended, and the remaining balance of $486.23 is now due in full. In addition, our car—the only car we have, which I rely on to commute to East Madison High School and transport our kids to school and appointments—needed unplanned repairs in November 2025 (brake pads and rotors, plus a battery replacement) totaling just over $700. We had no savings cushion left, so we put most of that on a high‑interest credit card.

The combination of these pandemic‑related debts and ongoing higher prices for groceries, gas, and utilities has left us with extremely tight monthly cash flow. I have tried to pick up additional work, including offering after‑school tutoring and teaching summer school in 2023 and 2024, but between my full‑time teaching responsibilities, IEP meetings, and caring for our children (our son receives speech therapy twice a week), there is a limit to how many extra hours I can realistically take on without burning out.

This loan would allow us to:
1. Pay off the overdue utility balance so we do not risk disconnection or additional fees.
2. Cover the remainder of the recent car repair bill so that we are not accruing more interest on our credit card.
3. Settle a small outstanding medical co‑pay from my post‑COVID follow‑up visit that has gone to collections and is currently affecting my credit.

In short, the pandemic created a long tail of financial strain that we are still working through despite being very careful with our spending. We are not asking for help to fund anything extravagant; this assistance would strictly go toward catching up on essential expenses—utilities, transportation, and medical bills—that are directly tied to the disruptions and health impacts we experienced during the COVID‑19 crisis. Having this loan repaid over time through payroll deduction would give us breathing room to get back on stable footing without having to choose between paying a utility bill and buying groceries in a given month.
reason_for_request.other_reason_for_request_description_of_:
In addition to the ongoing financial aftereffects of the pandemic, we have recently faced several other extenuating circumstances that have compounded our situation and made it difficult to get ahead.

First, our son Mateo was formally diagnosed with ADHD and a mild learning disability in reading this past spring. While I am very familiar with special education services through my work, actually navigating it as a parent has been eye‑opening. Although the school provides some services, we were strongly encouraged by his pediatrician and school psychologist to seek an outside evaluation and periodic follow‑up with a child psychologist. Our insurance covered only part of the initial evaluation, and we have had to pay approximately $75 per visit out‑of‑pocket for follow‑up appointments. We have already had four visits this year, and at least two more are recommended. We tried to space them out, but the bills still hit during months when money was already tight.

Second, our daughter Sofia has asthma, which has been more active this year. During the fall, she had two flare‑ups that required urgent care visits. Even with insurance, we had to pay co‑pays and for an updated inhaler and nebulizer supplies. These costs were not planned for and ended up on a credit card because we did not want to delay any of her care.

We also experienced a temporary loss of income this October when my husband’s employer cut everyone’s hours for nearly three weeks due to a slowdown in shipments at the warehouse. His hours were reduced from about 40 per week to 24–28, and his paycheck for that period was almost $300 less than usual. That shortfall had a domino effect: we postponed paying part of our credit card bill, which led to a late fee and additional interest; we also had to delay paying our internet bill until the very last day before disconnection, which added a small penalty.

On top of this, our rent increased by $110 per month when we renewed our lease in August 2025. We did shop around for other apartments, but moving costs (deposits, truck rental, lost work time) would have been even more expensive upfront, and many places were either farther from my school or in areas we did not feel comfortable raising our kids. We decided to stay put, but the higher rent has left almost no room in our monthly budget after paying for essentials.

Personally, this financial strain has taken an emotional toll on me as an educator. I often stay late at school preparing lessons and individualized supports for my students, many of whom come from families who are also struggling. I want to be fully present for them, but I find myself distracted by constant worry over which bill is due next and whether an unexpected expense—like a prescription refill or a flat tire—will push us over the edge. I have already sold a few personal items (an old guitar, some jewelry from my college days) and taken on extra duties where I can, such as covering classes during my prep period when colleagues are out, to earn a little extra pay.

We are not in crisis in the sense of facing immediate eviction, but we are in a precarious place where one more setback could make it very difficult to recover. This loan would not solve all of our long‑term financial challenges, but it would allow us to address the most pressing, high‑interest or overdue obligations right now, which in turn would free up a small amount of monthly income going forward. That breathing room would help us keep current on rent and utilities, continue our children’s medical and psychological care without interruption, and avoid relying even more heavily on high‑interest credit just to get through the month.
